figures.caption: Fig. 1: Overview of the EnTransformer architecture, consisting of noise injection, in-sample forecast generation, and optimization using the energy score loss. An ensemble of out-of-sample forecasts for all D nodes for the next q steps can be generated from the trained model by passing the p-length look-back window as input.

Table 2: Forecasting performance in terms of the CRPSsum metric (lower is better). Mean and standard error are computed from 10 independent evaluation runs. Results for the baseline models are taken from [4]. The best results are highlighted.
Fig. 10: Out-of-sample forecasts on the first 16 nodes of the Taxi dataset’s test window 4.
Fig. 11: Out-of-sample forecasts on the first 16 nodes of the Wikipedia dataset’s test window 1.
Fig. 6: Out-of-sample forecasts on the first 16 nodes of the Solar dataset’s test window 5.
Fig. 7: Out-of-sample forecasts on the first 16 nodes of the Electricity dataset’s test window 6.

Fig. 5: Ablation study analyzing the effect of the in-sample training ensemble size (M) on the proposed EnTransformer. (A) Computational overhead represented by the total training time in seconds; (B) Forecasting performance evaluated by the CRPSsum metric across the six datasets. The circular points represent the mean values, and the vertical error lines indicate the standard deviation computed over 10 independent runs.
Fig. 8: Out-of-sample forecasts on the first 16 nodes of the Traffic dataset’s test window 5.

Fig. 4: Probability Integral Transform (PIT) Q-Q plots evaluating the predictive calibration of EnTransformer across the six datasets. The empirical quantiles (solid blue) are plotted against the theoretical quantiles of an ideal uniform distribution, U(0, 1) (dashed black). The test window taken for each dataset corresponds to Figs. 6-11 in Appendix F.
Fig. 2: Forecasts produced by EnTransformer on selected nodes of the Traffic dataset on test window 5.
Fig. 9: Out-of-sample forecasts on the first 16 nodes of the KDD-cup dataset’s test window 1.

Table 1: Key characteristics of the benchmark datasets, including data granularity, dimension, number of training examples, context and prediction lengths, sequential lags, and number of rolling test windows used in this study.
Table 5: Forecasting performance in terms of the NRMSEsum metric (lower is better). Mean and standard error are computed from 10 independent evaluation runs. Best results are highlighted.
